title: Complete Guide: Hosting React + PostgreSQL on Local VMs with Internet Access
description: Learn to deploy a full-stack React application with PostgreSQL database on local VMs using VirtualBox. Master firewall configuration (iptables & Firewalld), SSH tunneling, port forwarding with TP-Link Deco router, and expose your app to the internet securely with HTTPS.
author: E-Learning Platform
heroAlt: Architecture diagram showing Public VM with React and Nginx, router with public IP, and Private VM with PostgreSQL database connected via SSH tunnel
tableOfContents: Part 2: Understanding Firewall Chains (INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD)
Part 7: TP-Link Deco Router Port Forwarding

sections.title: Architecture Overview
sections.description: Before diving into the technical details, let's understand the overall architecture of what we're building.

items.label: Network Setup Diagram
items.text:
Your Internet-facing setup will consist of:

• Your ISP provides a public IP (122.222.130.12)
• TP-Link Deco Router manages traffic and port forwarding
• Public VM (192.168.1.100) runs React + Nginx + SSH tunnel
• Private VM (192.168.1.101) runs PostgreSQL database
• Firewalls on both VMs restrict access
• SSH tunnel encrypts database communication

sections.title: Part 1: VM-to-VM Communication & Network Setup
sections.description: Setting up two isolated VMs that can communicate with each other.

items.label: VirtualBox Configuration
text: 3. Both VMs: Use Bridge Adapter for network mode
4. Install Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS on both

subsections.title: Setting Static IP Addresses
subsections.description: Configure static IPs for consistent connectivity.

items.label: Public VM Configuration
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo nano /etc/netplan/01-netcfg.yaml
# Edit with this content:
# network:
#   version: 2
#   ethernets:
#     eth0:
#       dhcp4: no
#       addresses: [192.168.1.100/24]
#       gateway4: 192.168.1.1
#       nameservers:
#         addresses: [8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4]

ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo netplan apply

items.label: Private VM Configuration
items.code:
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo nano /etc/netplan/01-netcfg.yaml
# Edit with this content:
# network:
#   version: 2
#   ethernets:
#     eth0:
#       dhcp4: no
#       addresses: [192.168.1.101/24]
#       gateway4: 192.168.1.1
#       nameservers:
#         addresses: [8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4]

ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo netplan apply

subsections.title: Testing VM-to-VM Communication
subsections.description: Verify both VMs can communicate with each other.

items.label: Ping Test
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ ping 192.168.1.101
PING 192.168.1.101 (192.168.1.101) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 192.168.1.101: icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.123 ms
64 bytes from 192.168.1.101: icmp_seq=2 ttl=64 time=0.115 ms

items.label: TCP Connectivity Test
items.code:
# On Private VM - start listener
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ nc -l -p 5432

# On Public VM - test connection
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ nc -zv 192.168.1.101 5432
Ncat: Version 7.80
Ncat: Connected to 192.168.1.101:5432

sections.title: Part 2: Understanding Firewall Chains
sections.description: Learn the three main firewall chains and their purposes.

items.label: Three Main iptables Chains
text: INPUT CHAIN: Controls traffic COMING INTO your system
OUTPUT CHAIN: Controls traffic GOING OUT from your system
FORWARD CHAIN: Controls traffic PASSING THROUGH your system (routing)

items.label: OUTPUT Chain - Outgoing Traffic
text: • Controls which external services your app can reach
• Manages API calls to payment gateways, mail servers, etc.

items.label: FORWARD Chain - Transit Traffic
text: • Used when your VM acts as a router/gateway

sections.title: Part 3: iptables Configuration
sections.description: Complete iptables setup for your public and private VMs.

items.label: Important Concept
items.text: iptables Rules are lost on reboot. We'll make them persistent using iptables-persistent.

subsections.title: Public VM Firewall Setup
subsections.description: Configure iptables on the Public VM to allow internet-facing traffic.

items.label: Complete Public VM Firewall Script
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo nano /usr/local/bin/firewall-public.sh

#!/bin/bash
# Flush existing rules
sudo iptables -F
sudo iptables -X

# Set default policies
sudo iptables -P INPUT DROP
sudo iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT
sudo iptables -P FORWARD DROP

# ========== INPUT CHAIN ==========
sudo iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A INPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p icmp -j ACCEPT

# ========== OUTPUT CHAIN ==========
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -o lo -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -p udp --dport 53 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp --dport 53 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp -d 192.168.1.101 --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp -d 192.168.1.101 --dport 5432 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

items.label: Make Executable
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo chmod +x /usr/local/bin/firewall-public.sh
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo bash /usr/local/bin/firewall-public.sh

subsections.title: Private VM Firewall Setup
subsections.description: Configure highly restrictive iptables on the Private VM.

items.label: Complete Private VM Firewall Script
items.code:
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo nano /usr/local/bin/firewall-private.sh

#!/bin/bash
# Flush existing rules
sudo iptables -F
sudo iptables -X

# Set default policies (deny by default)
sudo iptables -P INPUT DROP
sudo iptables -P OUTPUT DROP
sudo iptables -P FORWARD DROP

# ========== INPUT CHAIN ==========
sudo iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
# Allow SSH ONLY from Public VM
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -s 192.168.1.100 --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
# Allow PostgreSQL ONLY from Public VM
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -s 192.168.1.100 --dport 5432 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A INPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

# ========== OUTPUT CHAIN ==========
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -o lo -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp -d 192.168.1.100 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -p udp --dport 53 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp --dport 53 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

items.label: Run Private VM Firewall
items.code:
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo chmod +x /usr/local/bin/firewall-private.sh
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo bash /usr/local/bin/firewall-private.sh

subsections.title: Make iptables Rules Persistent
subsections.description: Save firewall rules so they survive reboots.

items.label: Install iptables-persistent
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo apt-get install iptables-persistent -y

# When prompted:
# Save IPv4 rules? Yes
# Save IPv6 rules? Yes

items.label: Save Current Rules
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo iptables-save | sudo tee /etc/iptables/rules.v4
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo netfilter-persistent save

sections.title: Part 4: Firewalld Configuration
sections.description: Alternative to iptables - simpler and more user-friendly.

items.label: When to Use Firewalld
items.text: Firewalld is easier to use and provides better management of complex rules. It's recommended for most production setups.

items.label: Installation
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo apt-get install firewalld -y
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl start firewalld
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl enable firewalld
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl status firewalld

items.label: Configure Public VM
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo firewall-cmd --set-default-zone=public
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=http
success
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=https
success
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=ssh
success

# Allow SSH to Private VM
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --add-rich-rule='rule family="ipv4" destination address="192.168.1.101" port protocol="tcp" port="22" accept'
success

ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo firewall-cmd --reload

items.label: Configure Private VM
items.code:
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo firewall-cmd --set-default-zone=dmz

# Allow SSH only from Public VM
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --add-rich-rule='rule family="ipv4" source address="192.168.1.100" service name="ssh" accept'
success

# Allow PostgreSQL only from Public VM
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --add-rich-rule='rule family="ipv4" source address="192.168.1.100" port protocol="tcp" port="5432" accept'
success

ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo firewall-cmd --reload

sections.title: Part 5: PostgreSQL Private Database Setup
sections.description: Install and configure PostgreSQL on the Private VM.

items.label: Install PostgreSQL
items.code:
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo apt-get update
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo apt-get install postgresql postgresql-contrib -y

ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo systemctl start postgresql
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo systemctl enable postgresql
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo systemctl status postgresql
PostgreSQL 14.8 (Ubuntu 14.8-1.pgdg22.04+1)

items.label: Step 1: Configure listen_addresses
items.code:
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo nano /etc/postgresql/14/main/postgresql.conf

# Find and change:
# BEFORE:
# #listen_addresses = 'localhost'
#
# AFTER:
# listen_addresses = '192.168.1.101'

items.label: Step 2: Update pg_hba.conf
items.code:
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo nano /etc/postgresql/14/main/pg_hba.conf

# Add at the end:
# Allow connection from Public VM only
host    all             all             192.168.1.100/32        md5

items.label: Step 3: Restart PostgreSQL
items.code: ubuntu@privatevm:~$ sudo systemctl restart postgresql

items.label: Create User and Database
Verify Connection from Public VM
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ psql -h 192.168.1.101 -U react_app -d react_db
Password for user react_app: StrongPassword123!

react_db=> SELECT version();
                      version                      
-----------
 PostgreSQL 14.8 (Ubuntu 14.8-1.pgdg22.04+1)

react_db=> \q

sections.title: Part 6: SSH Tunneling & Key Management
sections.description: Secure SSH tunneling for encrypted database communication.

items.label: Generate Key Pair on Windows
items.code:
PS C:\Users\YourName\.ssh> ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -f react_to_postgres -C "React VM to PostgreSQL"

Enter passphrase (empty for no passphrase): [Press Enter]
Enter same passphrase again: [Press Enter]

Your identification has been saved in react_to_postgres
Your public key has been saved in react_to_postgres.pub

subsections.title: Distribute Public Key to Private VM

items.label: Get Public Key Content
items.code:
PS C:\Users\YourName\.ssh> Get-Content react_to_postgres.pub
ssh-rsa AAAAB3NzaC1yc2EAAAADAQABAAACAQDf... react@windows

items.label: Add to Private VM authorized_keys
items.code:
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ mkdir -p ~/.ssh
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ nano ~/.ssh/authorized_keys

# Paste the public key content here
# Save: Ctrl+X → Y → Enter

ubuntu@privatevm:~$ chmod 700 ~/.ssh
ubuntu@privatevm:~$ chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys

subsections.title: Setup Private Key on Public VM

items.label: Copy Private Key to Public VM
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ mkdir -p ~/.ssh
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ nano ~/.ssh/react_to_postgres

# Paste the entire private key content
# Save: Ctrl+X → Y → Enter

ubuntu@publicvm:~$ chmod 700 ~/.ssh
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ chmod 600 ~/.ssh/react_to_postgres

items.label: Manual SSH Tunnel
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ ssh -i ~/.ssh/react_to_postgres \
    -L 5432:192.168.1.101:5432 \
    postgres@192.168.1.101 \
    -N -f

ubuntu@publicvm:~$ netstat -tln | grep 5432
tcp  0  0 127.0.0.1:5432  0.0.0.0:*  LISTEN

items.label: Persistent Tunnel (systemd)
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/postgres-tunnel.service

# Content:
# [Unit]
# Description=SSH Tunnel to PostgreSQL
# After=network.target
# 
# [Service]
# Type=simple
# User=root
# ExecStart=/usr/bin/ssh -i /root/.ssh/react_to_postgres -N -L 5432:192.168.1.101:5432 postgres@192.168.1.101
# Restart=always
# RestartSec=10
# 
# [Install]
# WantedBy=multi-user.target

ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl daemon-reload
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl enable postgres-tunnel.service
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl start postgres-tunnel.service

sections.title: Part 7: TP-Link Deco Router Port Forwarding
sections.description: Configure your router to forward internet traffic to your VM.

items.label: Critical Concept
items.text: Your local VMs are NOT automatically accessible from the internet. Port forwarding on your router makes them reachable by forwarding external traffic to internal IPs.

items.label: Get Public IP
items.code:
PS C:\> (Invoke-WebRequest -Uri 'http://ifconfig.me').Content
122.222.130.12

items.label: Router Access
text: Password: admin (default) or your custom password
3. Navigate to: Advanced → NAT → Port Forwarding

items.label: Verify Rules
items.text:
After creating rules, verify they appear in the Port Forwarding list:
✓ TCP 80 → 192.168.1.100:80
✓ TCP 443 → 192.168.1.100:443
✓ TCP 2222 → 192.168.1.100:22

sections.title: Part 8: Nginx Reverse Proxy & SSL/TLS
sections.description: Setup Nginx as reverse proxy with HTTPS support.

items.label: Why Nginx?
items.text: Nginx acts as a barrier between users and your app, providing security, caching, compression, and professional SSL/TLS management.

items.label: Install Nginx
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo apt-get update
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo apt-get install nginx -y
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl start nginx
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl enable nginx
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl status nginx

items.label: Install Node.js
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_18.x | sudo -E bash -
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo apt-get install -y nodejs
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ node --version
v18.16.0
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ npm --version
9.6.4

items.label: Create React App
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ mkdir -p ~/myapp && cd ~/myapp
ubuntu@publicvm:~/myapp$ npx create-react-app .

ubuntu@publicvm:~/myapp$ npm run build
> myapp@0.1.0 build
> react-scripts build
Compiled successfully!

items.label: Let's Encrypt (Free - Recommended)
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo apt-get install certbot python3-certbot-nginx -y

# If using domain from DuckDNS:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo certbot certonly --standalone -d myreactapp.duckdns.org -d www.myreactapp.duckdns.org

# Follow prompts for email and ToS acceptance

items.label: Self-Signed Certificate (for IP access)
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 \
    -keyout /etc/ssl/private/nginx-selfsigned.key \
    -out /etc/ssl/certs/nginx-selfsigned.crt

# When prompted:
# Country: PK
# State: Your State
# City: Your City
# Organization: My Company
# Common Name: 122.222.130.12

items.label: Edit Nginx Config
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo cp /etc/nginx/sites-available/default /etc/nginx/sites-available/default.bak
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo nano /etc/nginx/sites-available/default

items.label: Nginx Configuration (Full)
Test and Reload Nginx
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo nginx -t
nginx: the configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf syntax is ok
nginx: configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf test is successful

ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl reload nginx
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl status nginx

sections.title: Part 9: Dynamic DNS Setup
sections.description: Setup DuckDNS to handle ISP IP changes automatically.

items.label: Why Dynamic DNS?
items.text: Your ISP-assigned IP (122.222.130.12) might change when your router restarts. Dynamic DNS keeps your domain pointing to your current IP automatically.

items.label: Create Update Script
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ mkdir -p ~/scripts
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ nano ~/scripts/update-duckdns.sh

items.label: Script Content
items.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Update DuckDNS with current public IP

DOMAIN="myreactapp"
TOKEN="your-token-here"  # Get from duckdns.org

# Get current public IP
PUBLIC_IP=$(curl -s http://ifconfig.me)

# Update DuckDNS
curl "https://www.duckdns.org/update?domains=${DOMAIN}&token=${TOKEN}&ip=${PUBLIC_IP}"

# Log the update
echo "[$(date)] DuckDNS updated: IP = $PUBLIC_IP" >> /var/log/duckdns.log

items.label: Make Executable and Test
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ chmod +x ~/scripts/update-duckdns.sh
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ ~/scripts/update-duckdns.sh

items.label: Add to Crontab
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ crontab -e

# Add this line (runs every 5 minutes):
*/5 * * * * /home/ubuntu/scripts/update-duckdns.sh

items.label: Verify DNS Resolution
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ nslookup myreactapp.duckdns.org
Server: 8.8.8.8
Address: 8.8.8.8#53

Non-authoritative answer:
Name: myreactapp.duckdns.org
Address: 122.222.130.12

items.label: Test Order
items.text:
1. Local network access (same WiFi)
2. Port forwarding verification
3. Internet access from different network
4. Online port check tools
5. Check Nginx logs
6. Database connectivity

items.label: Test from Windows Machine
items.code:
PS C:\> curl -k https://192.168.1.100
# Should show React app HTML

PS C:\> Test-NetConnection -ComputerName 192.168.1.100 -Port 443
TcpTestSucceeded : True

items.label: Test from Different Network
text: 1. Use mobile hotspot or public WiFi

items.label: Online Port Check
text: 4. Should say: 'Success: I can see your service'

items.label: Monitor Nginx Logs
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log

# When accessing your site from internet, you'll see:
# 122.222.x.x - - [18/May/2024 10:30:45] "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 5234 "-"

sections.title: Part 11: Security Best Practices
sections.description: Harden your infrastructure for production use.

items.label: Security Checklist
text: ✓ Use strong passwords (20+ chars, mixed case, symbols)
✓ Keep PostgreSQL private (only accessible from Public VM)
✓ Regular backups of app and database
✓ Install Fail2Ban for brute force protection

items.label: Installation
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo apt-get install fail2ban -y
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl start fail2ban
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl enable fail2ban

items.label: Configuration
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo nano /etc/fail2ban/jail.local

# Content:
# [DEFAULT]
# bantime = 3600
# maxretry = 5
# 
# [sshd]
# enabled = true
# port = ssh
# filter = sshd
# logpath = /var/log/auth.log

items.label: Keep System Updated
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo apt-get update
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo apt-get upgrade -y
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo apt-get autoremove -y

items.label: Renew SSL Certificates
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo certbot renew --dry-run
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl enable certbot.timer
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl start certbot.timer

items.label: Backup React App
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo tar -czf ~/react-app-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz \
    /home/ubuntu/myapp/

items.label: Backup PostgreSQL Database
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo -u postgres pg_dump -Fc react_db > \
    ~/react-db-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).dump

sections.title: Troubleshooting Guide
sections.description: Common issues and how to fix them.

items.label: Solution 1: Verify Port Forwarding
items.text: Log into router (http://192.168.1.1) and verify port forwarding rules for 80 and 443 are enabled and pointing to 192.168.1.100

items.label: Solution 2: Check Nginx
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl status nginx
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo netstat -tlnp | grep :443

items.label: Solution 3: Check Firewall
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo ufw status
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo iptables -L INPUT -n | grep 443

items.label: Solution 1: Check React Build
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ ls -la /home/ubuntu/myapp/build/
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ cd ~/myapp && npm run build

items.label: Solution 2: Check Nginx Config
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo nginx -t
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl restart nginx

items.label: Solution 3: Check Error Logs
items.code: ubuntu@publicvm:~$ tail -f /var/log/nginx/error.log

items.label: Problem
items.text: React app can't connect to PostgreSQL

items.label: Solution 1: Check SSH Tunnel
items.code:
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ netstat -tln | grep 5432
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ ps aux | grep ssh

items.label: Solution 2: Test PostgreSQL
items.code: ubuntu@publicvm:~$ psql -h localhost -U react_app -d react_db -c "SELECT 1"

items.label: System Verification
items.code:
# Check all services
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl status nginx
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo systemctl status ssh

# Check listening ports
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo netstat -tlnp | grep -E ':(22|80|443|5432)'

# Check firewall
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ sudo ufw status

# Test local access
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ curl http://192.168.1.100
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ curl -k https://192.168.1.100

# Test database
ubuntu@publicvm:~$ psql -h localhost -U react_app -d react_db
